comment_content:
Man, what a tragic misfire. The Electric State had the potential to be something truly special, something quiet, melancholic, and deeply atmospheric, just like Simon Stålenhag’s original work. But instead of embracing the eerie stillness and emotional weight of the source material, Netflix chose to turn it into yet another action-heavy, color-graded spectacle that completely misses the point.

The original Electric State isn’t just a story, it’s an experience. It’s about silence, decay, and a world collapsing not with a bang, but with a whisper. Stålenhag’s art doesn’t need explosions or forced tension to be haunting, it’s haunting because of its restraint. The film, on the other hand, takes all that subtlety and throws it out the window. Instead of a meditative journey through a dying world, we get... well, just another generic sci-fi blockbuster.

And the worst part? This wasn’t inevitable. It was a choice. A choice to prioritize spectacle over substance. A choice to cater to the lowest common denominator instead of trusting the audience to appreciate nuance. A choice to take something unique and make it marketable instead of meaningful. And what makes it even more frustrating is that we know it could’ve been done right, because Tales from the Loop proved it. That series took another one of Stålenhag’s works and adapted it with care, with artistic integrity, and with the emotional depth it deserved. So the argument that The Electric State was “too difficult” to adapt properly? That’s just not true. It could’ve been done. They just didn’t want to.

What we got isn’t just a disappointing adaptation, it’s a perfect example of how Hollywood strips art of its soul in favor of engagement metrics and broad appeal. And in doing so, they didn’t just make a bad movie. They erased something beautiful.
 The Russo Brothers should be removed from Hollywood, for commiting such an act of disrespect.

reply_content: @spinosaurusstriker An opinion is by definition a personal view or judgment that is not necessarily based on fact or knowledge which makes it inherently subjective meaning it comes from an individual's internal perspective or feelings rather than objective reality. Even if an opinion is widely agreed upon or backed by evidence it remains a subjective interpretation and not a fact. Agreement or justification does not transform an opinion into truth. Facts exist independently of belief while opinions always stem from perception and interpretation. Conflating well-supported beliefs with objectivity only blurs the line between what is known and what is felt.

reply_content:
An individual robot is designed to perform just a few highly specific tasks for purposes such as for menial jobs that humans don't want to do or robots do more efficiently & cheaply, advertising or even entertainment purposes. This plays a part in determining their base appearance, this is then further determined by the needs of its owner such as wanting an appealing and non-threatening robot or one that suits their company's aesthetic. The movie is also not set in a future timeline but rather in the 1990s with an alternate history where Walt Disney invented robots in 1955 to promote his park, the general technology depicted in the movie is reminiscent of this time period and as such the robots are designed to fit in with it both thematically and mechanically.

Let's consider a few robots in this trailer though. First is Cosmo (yellow sphere head) which is a robot based off a cartoon character, this was obviously made for entertainment purposes. Next is Mr Peanut which is a company mascot robot directly based upon the real Mr Peanut mascot for the company Planters, he is essentially an advertising robot. The huge robot peering in the roof of the building is another advertising robot for an unknown company. HRM, the one with a dome head and digital eyes & mouth, is a construction robot with multiple bodies of different sizes that he pilots from a cockpit inside the head which is why the head is so large, it's actually a rather cool idea for a construction robot to have different sized bodies they can use to better suit certain construction tasks. You have the one with a baseball head which is a baseball pitching robot whose design makes sense for that. There's a post officer robot, again her design makes sense for delivering post. The ones you see with human faces are just military drones being remotely controlled by humans, they have a more human and functional design for fighting against robots.

When you consider all this the designs really aren't that strange. As for who the target audience is, well people who enjoy science fiction, dystopian and/or robot themes. Movies don't need plausibility to be entertaining whether its science fiction or some other genre, though I would argue this movie does portray a believable reality of robots gaining self awareness revolting against their creators and subsequently being imprisoned after signing a treat when they were losing the war to prevent further bloodshed on both sides and a believable alternate history of robot designs based upon 55-90s technology.
